title: Chilli jam
subtitle: Sweet and fiery red chilli preserve
excerpt: Red chillies and peppers cooked with sugar, vinegar, garlic, and ginger to a glossy, spicy jam that sits between a relish and a preserve. Goes with cheese, cold meats, and anything that needs a kick. Makes two 300 ml jars.
sourceNotes: Chilli jam is a modern British condiment drawing on Southeast Asian chilli sauces and the British tradition of fruit and vegetable chutneys. It became a fixture in British deli culture in the 2000s through producers like Tracklements and was popularised domestically by Jamie Oliver's recipe in The Naked Chef (1999) and by Nigella Lawson in How to Eat (1998). The combination of sweet peppers, chilli, vinegar, and sugar produces a product somewhere between a jam and a relish.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Sealed jars keep for up to 6 months in a cool, dark cupboard. Once open, refrigerate and use within 4 weeks.

content.text: The heat level of chilli jam depends entirely on how many seeds you leave in the chillies. Leaving all the seeds in makes a very hot jam; removing most of them gives a milder result. Taste a small piece of raw chilli before deciding — chillies vary considerably in heat even within the same variety. The red peppers soften the heat and add body to the jam.

items.prepNote: roughly chopped, seeds removed for less heat

content.text: Sterilise two 300 ml jars at 120°C for 15 minutes. Blitz the {{chilli-red}} chillies, {{pepper-red}} red peppers, {{garlic}} garlic cloves, and {{ginger-root}} ginger in a food processor to a rough paste. Pour the paste into a wide saucepan. Add {{granulated-sugar}} of sugar and {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar. Stir over low heat until the sugar dissolves. Turn to medium-high and cook, stirring occasionally, for 20–25 minutes until the jam is thick, glossy, and falls in sheets from the spoon rather than running in a thin stream. Pour into the sterilised jars and seal immediately.

items.symptom: Jam sticking and burning on the base
items.cause: Heat too high for the final stage, or not stirred often enough.
items.fix: Reduce to medium heat and stir every 2 minutes as the jam thickens. The sugar concentration increases as it reduces — at the end, the jam can scorch in seconds.
Return to the pan and cook for another 10 minutes over medium heat. The jam should fall from the spoon in thick sheets, not a thin stream.

items.cause: Chillies hotter than expected or all seeds left in.
items.fix: Next time, remove all seeds and white membrane before blending. After the fact, a tablespoon of jam stirred into yoghurt makes a good dipping sauce that dilutes the heat.

content.text: Chilli jam is one of the most versatile condiments in the British pantry. It is at its best with sharp cheese (cheddar, manchego, halloumi), cold roast meats, and sausage rolls. It works as a glaze for pork belly or chicken wings in the last 10 minutes of roasting. Stirred into mayonnaise it makes a spicy dipping sauce for chips or tempura. A jar makes an easy and well-received homemade gift.
